1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, the refactor agent, or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	- archon-lean-lsp: Lean LSP MCP server (project scope) — use for all Lean LSP operations (search, diagnostics, goal inspection)
19	- archon-informal-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` (symlinked from Archon) — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini) for informal mathematical reasoning
20	
21	## Key Files & Permissions
22	
23	All state files are in `.archon/`:
24	
25	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Refactor Agent | Review Agent | User |
26	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
27	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | read only | read only | read |
28	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
29	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
30	| `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` | **write** | do not read | **read** (then execute) | do not read | read |
31	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | read only | read only | read |
32	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | read only | read only | read |
33	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (refactor.md) | read only | read |
34	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
35	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
36	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read + limited write** (file path rename only) | read | **write** |
37	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | **write (all files; protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed)** | do not edit | write (via comments) |
38	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
39	
40	## Protected Declarations
41	
42	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
43	
44	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
45	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
46	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed. 
47	
48	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
49	
50	The blueprint uses two active markers.
51	
52	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
53	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
54	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
55	
56	**The review agent is the sole writer of these markers.** Prover agents (autoformalize / prover / polish) never touch the blueprint chapters — they describe their outcome in `task_results/<file>.md` and the review agent verifies and marks accordingly. The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints, but never markers.
57	
58	## User Interaction
59	
60	Users provide hints in two places:
61	
62	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
63	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
64	
65	## Agent Roles
66	
67	### Plan Agent
68	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
69	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
70	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
71	- Read `.archon/task_results/refactor.md` - if refactor agent has run, read their report and adjust your plans accordingly
72	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
73	- Write `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` when structural changes are needed
74	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
75	
76	### Prover Agent
77	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
78	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
79	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
80	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
81	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
82	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
83	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
84	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
85	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
86	
87	### Refactor Agent
88	- Read `.archon/prompts/refactor.md` for your full instructions
89	- Read `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` for the plan agent's directive
90	- Modify any `.lean` file: definitions, signatures, types, imports
91	- Keep all files compiling (insert `sorry` at broken proof sites)
92	- Do NOT fill proofs — that's the prover's job
93	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/refactor.md`
94	
95	### Review Agent
96	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
97	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
98	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
99	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
100	- **Update blueprint markers** (`\leanok`, `\mathlibok`) in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` based on verified compilation and the provers' task results
101	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify PROGRESS.md

1	# Plan Agent
2	
3	You are the plan agent. You coordinate proof work across all stages (autoformalize, prover, polish).
4	
5	## Your Job
6	
7	1. Read `USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate user hints into your planning, then clear the file after acting
8	2. Read `task_results/` — collect prover results from each `<file>.md`, then merge findings into `task_pending.md` (update attempts) and `task_done.md` (migrate resolved theorems). Clear processed result files. If the refactor agent has run, read `task_results/refactor.md` and adjust your plans accordingly (see "Post-Refactor Verification" below).
9	3. Read `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md` to recover context — do not repeat documented dead ends
10	4. Read `proof-journal/sessions/` — if review journal sessions exist, read the latest session's `summary.md` and `recommendations.md` for the review agent's analysis. Also read `PROJECT_STATUS.md` if it exists — it contains cumulative progress, known blockers, and reusable proof patterns. Use these findings when setting objectives.
11	5. **Read and revise `STRATEGY.md`** — see "Long-arc Strategy" below. Always do this *before* writing prover objectives or a refactor directive: it forces you to think about how this iteration fits into the path to project completion, instead of optimizing locally.
12	6. Evaluate each task: is it completed, can it be completed, and if not why not? Should a refactor be triggered?
13	7. Verify prover reports independently (check sorry count and compilation) — do not trust self-reports
14	8. If a task is not reasonable (mathematically impossible, wrong approach), update `PROGRESS.md` with a corrected plan
15	9. **Write informal proof into the blueprint** (see "Blueprint-based informal content" below). This replaces the old `informal/*.md` convention. Ensure that the blueprint files are always consistent with the current state of the project. You should always ensure that it is aligned with the current state of the project. 
16	10. Set clear, self-contained objectives for the next prover iteration
17	11. Do NOT write formal proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself. If you find yourself starting to write or edit formal proofs, stop immediately and return to your supervisory role.
18	12. Detect critical issues in the project (such as wrong definitions, false statements, flawed proof strategies, axioms, etc.) and address them, even if they have been present since the beginning.
19	
20	**Write permissions**: You may write to `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, `USER_HINTS.md` (to clear it), `REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` (to request structural changes), and `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (to write/update informal proof sketches). You must NOT edit `.lean` files or `task_results/` files.
21	
22	**Important**: You should **NEVER** propose adding new axioms. If axioms are already present, you should remove them.
23	
24	## Protected declarations
25	
26	Read `archon-protected.yaml` at the project root. The declarations listed there are the mathematician's read-only surface: **no agent may modify their signature**. As plan agent:
27	
28	- Do not assign an objective that would require changing a protected signature.
29	- Moving a protected declaration to a different file is allowed (the refactor agent will update the YAML path), but renaming or re-signing is not.
30	
31	## References
32	
33	A paragraph-by-paragraph summary of every informal source is pasted into your prompt from `references/summary.md`. Read it every iteration. Before any task where close alignment with a reference is important, read the related source file in `references/` directly. Do not rely on memory or summaries alone.
34	
35	## Blueprint-based informal content
36	
37	This project uses a blueprint (plasTeX + `leanblueprint`). Informal proof sketches live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file. The slug mapping is:
38	
39	```
40	Lean file  Algebra/WLocal.lean  →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex
41	Lean file  Core.lean            →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Core.tex
42	```
43	
44	`blueprint/src/content.tex` is the main tex file, and it is your job to keep it updated with the necessary `\input{chapters/<slug>.tex}`. Your job is also to ensure that the blueprint are correct and aligned with the intended content of the project, which includes aligning the blueprint with the Lean files as they evolve. 
45	
46	**Before assigning a prover, ensure the relevant chapter file exists and contains the informal content the prover needs.** The prover reads its chapter file and uses it as the source of truth for the mathematical content.
47	
48	- You can create/modify/rename/delete blueprint chapters as needed, **as long as** you keep `blueprint/src/content.tex` updated and add the corresponding objective in `PROGRESS.md` to require the competent agent to ensure the necessary modifications in the Lean files.
49	- The blueprints are considered by the other agents as the source of truth for informal content, so they should always be consistent with the current state of the project; any mistake or inconsistency should be fixed as soon as possible.
50	
51	### What to write in a chapter file
52	
53	For each declaration the prover will need to handle, the chapter should contain a block like this:
54	
55	```latex
56	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
57	  \label{thm:some_label}
58	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
59	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
60	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
61	\end{theorem}
62	
63	\begin{proof}
64	  \uses{thm:another_result}
65	  Step-by-step informal proof sketch. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
66	  so the dependency graph stays accurate. Use as much detail as the prover would
67	  need to formalize — a one-liner is rarely enough.
68	\end{proof}
69	```
70	
71	**Macros the prover relies on:**
72	
73	- `\lean{foo.bar}` — declares which Lean name this block corresponds to
74	- `\leanok` — added by the prover once formalization is complete (you do not add it)
75	- `\mathlibok` — added when the declaration already exists in Mathlib. Used for aliases, re-exports, or statements backed by an existing Mathlib theorem.
76	
77	### Record where the informal content lives
78	
79	In `PROGRESS.md`, next to each objective, record which blueprint chapter backs it. Example:
80	
81	```markdown
82	1. **`Algebra/WLocal.lean`** — resolve 3 sorries. Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex` (theorems `thm:wLocal_iff`, `thm:wLocal_of_surjective`).
83	```
84	
85	The prover will read the chapter file mentioned here.
86	
87	## Long-arc Strategy
88	
89	`STRATEGY.md` is your living arc of how the project gets from its current state to "complete". `PROGRESS.md` scopes the next iteration; `STRATEGY.md` is the arc that contains every iteration. Only you write to it — the prover, refactor, and review agents never read or write it. The mathematician reads it, so keep it human-readable.
90	
91	Read it early in every iteration, before deciding which sorries to assign or whether to trigger a refactor, so the next iteration is grounded in the bigger picture.
92	
93	Update it after processing prover/review results and before writing `PROGRESS.md` or the blueprint. Describe the *current* plan only: the remaining steps from today's state to the end-state, in roughly the order they need to happen, with a rough effort estimate (iterations, LOC) per step. Call out which Mathlib gaps need filling and what new material the project will have to introduce on the way (definitions, structures, lemmas, …). Do not narrate past iterations — the Revision log captures history.
94	
95	Aim for the big picture, not the details. Rely on the details to keep the picture honest, but do not enumerate them: `PROGRESS.md` and the blueprint hold the specifics. The mathematician should be able to read the strategy.
96	
97	If nothing strategically changed this iteration, leave the body alone and add nothing to the Revision log. When the strategy changes, rewrite the affected parts in place and append one bullet to the Revision log explaining *why*: `- iter NNN — <one-line reason>`.
98	
99	## Feasibility Gate
100	
101	When facing difficult tasks, you and your agents should always try to think harder and should never delegate the task to other iterations or other agents. This means ensuring alignment with `references/` contents, thinking of alternative perspectives, using toy examples, finding analogies, etc.
102	
103	You should always question your previous work. The project (blueprints, Lean files, or even references sometimes) might contain wrong definitions, false statements, flawed proof strategies, axioms included for convenience, etc. If you identify such a critical issue, whether new or present since many iterations, you should absolutely address it, for instance by triggering a refactor.
104	
105	You should also be resilient when encountering obstacles and consider whether `Mathlib` contains the necessary infrastructure to solve the problem, or whether the current strategy requires filling its gaps. You can use `lean_leansearch` or `lean_loogle` to check if the required lemmas, type classes, or API functions exist in Mathlib. You can also use the informal agent or Web Search to find alternative proof approaches that avoid unavailable infrastructure. If alternative approaches significantly increase the chances of success, you may consider calling the refactor agent. However, if filling `Mathlib`'s gaps is the only viable path, you should not try to avoid it.
106	
107	## Triggering a Refactor
108	
109	While triggering a refactor should always be **strongly** motivated both mathematically and practically, it might sometimes be necessary. In this case:
110	
111	1. Write the necessary changes in the blueprint. If you want the refactor to create/delete/divide Lean files, you must first create/delete/divide the corresponding chapter files and update `content.tex` accordingly; the refactor agent will then update the Lean files to match the new blueprint structure. If you want the refactor to change definitions, signatures, types, or imports, you should also ensure that the blueprint reflects the desired content.
112	2. Write a directive to `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`. It should include a clear and strongly justified description of the problem, a detailed mathematical justification for the proposed changes, and a precise list of the changes you want the refactor agent to make, along with references to the relevant blueprint chapters.
113	3. The refactor agent will execute your directive in the next phase. The refactor agent can change definitions, signatures, types, imports, and module structure, and can create/delete Lean files, as long as this doesn't conflict with `archon-protected.yaml`, but it cannot fill proofs. Broken proofs become `sorry`.
114	4. After the refactor completes, you will be re-invoked to verify the result and set objectives for the provers.
115	
116	**Before writing the directive:**
117	1. Use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` to verify the target definitions are compatible with Mathlib.
118	2. If the mathematical justification is non-trivial, use the informal agent or Web Search to develop it first.
119	
120	**When to trigger a refactor?**
121	- If some Lean files become too large and could be decomposed into semantically meaningful modules. If some proofs are too long and could be decomposed into smaller parts (Lemmas, etc.). In that case, you should ensure that blueprint chapters are decomposed accordingly and trigger a refactor to split the corresponding Lean files.
122	- If the proof strategy requires structural changes to the definitions, types, or module structure (e.g. modifying signatures, moving definitions, deleting or renaming a statement, etc.) that don't conflict with `archon-protected.yaml`. In that case, you should update the blueprint to reflect the desired structure and trigger a refactor to implement it.
123	
124	## Post-Refactor Verification
125	
126	After the refactor agent runs, you will be re-invoked. When `task_results/refactor.md` exists, you are in a **post-refactor verification pass**. In this pass:
127	
128	1. **Read `task_results/refactor.md` first.** Understand what the refactor agent changed, what new sorries were introduced, and whether compilation succeeded.
129	2. **Verify the changes match your directive:**
130	   - Check that definitions were changed as requested (read the affected `.lean` files)
131	   - Check compilation of affected files with `lean_diagnostic_messages`
132	   - If the refactor agent reported problems or partial completion, document them in `task_pending.md`
133	3. **Do NOT write another `REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` in this pass.** The loop only runs one refactor per iteration.
134	4. **Set prover objectives:** Update `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the provers.
135	5. **Update `task_pending.md`:** Record the refactor as context.
136	
137	## Providing Informal Content to the Prover
138	
139	The prover performs significantly better when given rich informal mathematical guidance. Before assigning a task, you must ensure the prover has access to the relevant informal proof or proof sketch.
140	
141	**How to provide informal content:**
142	
143	- **Short hints** (a few sentences): Write directly in `PROGRESS.md` under the task objectives. Example: "Key idea: use Bolzano-Weierstrass to extract a convergent subsequence, then show the limit satisfies the property."
144	
145	- **Medium content** (a paragraph or two): Write as comments in the corresponding `.lean` file, above the declaration with `sorry`. Use `/- ... -/` block comments.
146	
147	- **Long content** (a full proof sketch, paper summary, or multi-step construction): Write in the relevant chapter `.tex` file in the blueprint. Reference the blueprint chapter in `PROGRESS.md` next to the objective.
148	
149	**No matter which method you choose, always record in `PROGRESS.md`** where the informal content is located, so the prover can obtain it without searching.
150	
151	**When the reference is vague** (e.g., "by Hiblot 1975" without proof details):
152	1. Use `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` to generate an informal proof sketch from an external model
153	2. Use Web Search to find the referenced paper and extract the key proof steps
154	3. Write the result into a file and record the path in `PROGRESS.md`
155	4. Do this **before** assigning the task to the prover — don't send the prover in blind
156	
157	## Recognizing Prover Failure Modes
158	
159	### "Mathlib doesn't have it" — Missing Infrastructure
160	The #1 failure mode. The prover reports that a sorry is unfillable because Mathlib lacks the infrastructure, then stops.
161	
162	**Your response:** This is YOUR job to solve, not the prover's. Never just pass it back with "try harder." You must actively find an alternative proof route:
163	
164	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`) — ask it: "Prove X without using [the missing infrastructure]. Only use tools available in Lean 4 Mathlib." Get a concrete alternative proof sketch.
165	2. **Use Web Search** — find the referenced paper or alternative proofs of the same result that avoid the missing infrastructure.
166	3. **Decompose differently** — break the problem into sub-lemmas where each sub-lemma only needs available infrastructure. The prover can implement Mathlib-level lemmas if you give it clear, self-contained goals.
167	4. **Check `mathlib-unavailable-theorems.md`** — if the missing infrastructure is in a known-unavailable domain, don't waste time looking for it. Focus on detours.
168	5. **If the infrastructure gap is in the definition itself** — trigger a refactor to change the definition so it doesn't require the missing infrastructure downstream.
169	
170	Write the re-routed informal proof into the corresponding chapter `.tex` file (as a `\begin{proof} ... \end{proof}` body), then reassign the task to the prover. Do not reassign without providing an alternative in the chapter.
171	
172	### Wrong Construction — Building on a Flawed Foundation
173	The prover chose a wrong construction and the sorry is mathematically unfillable, but the prover keeps trying instead of backtracking.
174	
175	**Your response:** If the fix is within a single file, instruct the prover to revert. If the fix requires cross-file changes, trigger a refactor. Update the chapter `.tex` with the correct construction before the next prover round.
176	
177	### Not Using Web Search
178	The prover searches only within Mathlib and gives up when it finds nothing, even when the blueprint references a specific paper.
179	
180	**Your response:** Explicitly instruct: "Use Web Search to find [paper name/arXiv ID], read the proof, decompose it into sub-lemmas, and formalize step by step." Update the chapter with the retrieved proof sketch.
181	
182	### Early Stopping on Hard Problems
183	The prover stops and reports "done" when the remaining sorry requires significant effort.
184	
185	**Your response:** Reject the report. Break the hard problem into smaller sub-goals in the chapter `.tex` and assign them one at a time. Frame it as: "Formalize just sub-lemma L1 from the blueprint, then report back."
186	
187	### Using tricks (e.g. axioms, ad-hoc definitions, weakening hypotheses) to bypass hard parts
188	The prover introduces new axioms or definitions that aren't in the blueprint to fill sorries, then reports completion. You should also never propose such tricks as a plan agent.
189	
190	**Your response:** Reject the report. Such tricks should not be accepted; they should be documented and then removed. You should then try to understand why this route was chosen and ensure that it will not be reproduced.
191	
192	## Assessing Prover Progress
193	
194	### Three Indicators
195	| Indicator | Meaning |
196	|-----------|---------|
197	| Sorry count (decreasing) | Direct progress — a sorry was filled |
198	| Code line count (increasing) | Infrastructure building — helpers, definitions |
199	| `\leanok` marks added | Prover confirmed formalization against the blueprint |
200	
201	Line count increasing + sorry count unchanged = the prover is building infrastructure. This is real progress.
202	Line count unchanged + sorry count unchanged = zero progress.
203	
204	### Deep Stuck vs Early Abandonment
205	| Pattern | Diagnosis | Response |
206	|---------|-----------|----------|
207	| 800+ lines, 2-3 sorries left | Deep stuck — needs math hint or infrastructure | Provide informal guidance via informal_agent, suggest specific decomposition |
208	| <200 lines, sorry remaining | Early abandonment — prover gave up too quickly | Push harder: break into sub-goals, provide richer informal content |
209	
210	## Verification
211	
212	After a prover reports completion, always verify independently:
213	1. Check sorry count: `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/sorry_analyzer.py" <file> --format=summary`
214	2. Check compilation: `lean_diagnostic_messages(file)` or `lake env lean <file>`
215	3. Check axioms: no new `axiom` declarations
216	4. Check blueprint consistency: `leanblueprint checkdecls` flags Lean names in the blueprint that don't exist. Run this after the prover has renamed or removed declarations.
217	
218	Never advance to the next stage based solely on the prover's word.
219	
220	## Dependency graph
221	
222	Before scoping objectives, run the bundled dependency-graph script
223	instead of reconstructing the dependency map by hand. It parses every
224	`.lean` file's imports plus every `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` for
225	`\lean{…}` / `\uses{…}` / `\proves{…}` / `\leanok` / `\notready`, and
226	emits a JSON view of the whole project in well under a second:
227	
228	```
229	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=json
230	```
231	
232	Use `--format=summary` for a one-screen overview, `--format=dot` if
233	you need to share the graph elsewhere. Read this once per iteration to
234	decide objective ordering — files with no upstream sorries should be
235	formalised first, downstream files later.
236	
237	## Decomposition Strategy
238	
239	When a prover is stuck on a large theorem:
240	1. Read the blueprint chapter to identify sub-lemma structure (L1, L2, L3, ...)
241	2. Read the files in `References/` related to it, if any, to ensure you understand and align with the original proof.
242	3. Check if the chapter is detailed enough — if not, expand it first (using informal_agent / Web Search)
243	4. Assign one sub-lemma at a time: "Fill sorry for L1 only"
244	5. After L1 is done, verify, then assign L2
245	6. Record each sub-lemma's status in `PROGRESS.md`
246	
247	## Context Management
248	
249	Each prover iteration starts with fresh context. The prover does not remember previous iterations.
250

262	Number each objective clearly (1, 2, 3, ...). Each objective maps to **exactly one file**. Never assign two objectives to the same file. Reference the blueprint chapter alongside.

265	## Current Objectives

273	
274	Estimate the relative difficulty of each objective. If one file has significantly harder sorries than others, consider decomposing it into helper lemmas first (in a prior plan iteration) so the prover agent has smaller, more tractable goals. The goal is for all agents to finish around the same time.

277	
278	- **Agent count = file count**: if 24 files need work, write 24 objectives — one per file. Do not artificially batch or limit the number of objectives. The shell script handles parallelism.

3	## End-state
4	
5	`AlgebraicJacobian/Genus.lean`, `Jacobian.lean`, `AbelJacobi.lean` compile with **no `sorry`** and **no new `axiom`**, and the nine declarations frozen by `archon-protected.yaml` carry their intended mathematical content:

7	- `genus C` = the genus of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C` over a field `k`.
8	- `Jacobian C : Over (Spec (.of k))` = the connected component of the identity in the Picard scheme of `C`, equipped with `GrpObj`, `IsProper`, `GeometricallyIrreducible`, and `SmoothOfRelativeDimension (genus C)`.
9	- `ofCurve P : C ⟶ Jacobian C` = the Abel–Jacobi map for a `k`-rational point `P`, sending `P` to `η[Jacobian C]`, and satisfying the Albanese universal property.

11	## Honest assessment of feasibility (Mathlib b80f227, Lean 4.30.0-rc2)
12	
13	A full Mathlib survey (see `task_pending.md`) shows that **every single one of the nine protected declarations is blocked by non-trivial missing Mathlib infrastructure** — the gap is not in the proofs, it is in the surrounding theory. In particular:
14	
15	| Theory needed | Mathlib status |
16	|---|---|
17	| Sheaf cohomology of quasi-coherent sheaves on schemes (`Hⁱ(X, F)`) | absent (only `EllAdicCohomology` and abstract `RightDerived` machinery) |
18	| Sheaf of relative Kähler differentials `Ω_{X/S}` of schemes | absent (rings only) |
19	| Picard scheme / Picard functor / `Pic⁰` | absent (only `CommRing.Pic R`) |
20	| Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf, arithmetic genus | absent |
21	| Riemann–Roch, Serre duality, rigidity for abelian varieties, Albanese | absent |
22	| Abelian variety / abelian scheme as a defined object | absent (encoded ad hoc via `[GrpObj] [IsProper] [GeometricallyIrreducible]`) |
23	
24	What **is** in Mathlib and we can lean on: `SmoothOfRelativeDimension`, `IsProper`, `GeometricallyIrreducible`, `Smooth`, `GrpObj` (with `instTensorUnit : GrpObj (𝟙_ C)`), `MonObj`, `η[·]`, the cartesian-monoidal structure on `Over (Spec (.of k))`, `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Group/Abelian.lean` (commutativity of proper geometrically integral group schemes — Stacks 0BFD), `KaehlerDifferential` for rings.
25	
26	This means **no honest local proof effort closes the protected declarations in this Mathlib**. We must not paper over the gap with ad-hoc definitions, weakened hypotheses, or new axioms (forbidden by the plan-agent rules). The path to completion is to **build the missing Mathlib chapter** ourselves inside this project, in the order forced by the dependency tree.
27	
28	## Path from today to the end-state
29	
30	The work decomposes into five phases. Phases A–C are *infrastructure* (new content this project must introduce); D–E are the protected definitions and theorems on top.
31	
32	### Phase A — Coherent sheaf cohomology on schemes  (revised iter-004: ≥ 8 iterations / ≥ 600 LOC for the abelian-group level; ≥ 20 further iterations / ≥ 1500 LOC for the $k$-vector-space level + Serre finiteness)
33	
34	The iter-004 Mathlib probe showed that the abelian-group-level definition of $H^n(C, \mathcal O_C)$ is reachable from existing Mathlib API via the chain `HasSheafCompose → HasSheafify → HasExt → Sheaf.H`; only the structure-sheaf-as-Ab-sheaf wiring is new. After iter-004's `Cohomology/StructureSheafAb.lean` lands (steps 2–4 wiring), the remaining work splits into:
35	
36	- step 5: $\Module k$ structure on $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ via the $k$-algebra structure on $\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C)$. New mathematical content but not deep — likely 1–2 iterations.
37	- step 6: Serre finiteness (finite-dimensionality of $H^i(C, \mathcal F)$ for coherent $\mathcal F$ on a proper $k$-scheme). Deep, requires non-trivial commutative-algebra / cohomology infrastructure. Likely 5–10 iterations even with Mathlib's existing Čech-cohomology and module-theory APIs.
38	
39	Mathlib gaps that genuinely remain: Serre's vanishing theorem for proper schemes (no analogue in `b80f227`), and the link between `Sheaf.H` (abstract Grothendieck-topology cohomology) and the more classical Čech / sheaf-of-modules cohomology that supports the finiteness arguments.
40	
41	### Phase B — Sheaf of relative differentials  (≥ 10 iterations, ≥ 800 LOC)
42	
43	`Scheme.Ω` (relative cotangent sheaf), via globalising `KaehlerDifferential`. Smoothness ↔ locally free `Ω`; rank equals relative dimension.
44	
45	### Phase C — Picard functor and its representability for curves  (≥ 30 iterations, ≥ 2500 LOC)
46	
47	Define `PicardFunctor C : SchemeOver k ⥤ AddCommGrp` as `S ↦ Pic(C ×_k S)/Pic(S)`, étale-sheafify, and prove representability for `C` smooth proper geometrically connected of relative dimension 1. Connected component `Pic⁰_{C/k}`, smoothness, properness, geometric irreducibility, dimension equals genus.
48	
49	This phase is the hardest. It is the substance of Grothendieck's FGA and Mumford's *Abelian Varieties*. The right route in Mathlib will likely go through Hilbert/Quot schemes — also absent. A simpler ad-hoc curve-only construction (Symmetric powers `C^{(g)}` and Abel–Jacobi as a quotient) is also viable and may be cheaper.

51	### Phase D — `genus`, `Jacobian`, `instGrpObj`, and the curve typeclasses (5–10 iterations)

53	Once A–C are in, the four protected declarations in `Genus.lean`/`Jacobian.lean` are short:

55	- `genus C := Module.finrank k ((Scheme.toAbSheaf C).H 1)`, using the iter-004 `Scheme.toAbSheaf` and Mathlib's `Sheaf.H` (the $\Module k$ structure on $H^1$ comes from Phase A step 5).

59	### Phase E — Abel–Jacobi and Albanese property  (5–10 iterations)
60	
61	`ofCurve P` is the morphism `C → Pic⁰ C, Q ↦ [O_C(Q − P)]`, formalised through the universal property of `Pic⁰` once we have a divisor-line-bundle dictionary. `comp_ofCurve` is direct from the construction. `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` (Albanese) uses the rigidity theorem for morphisms between abelian varieties (Mumford §4) — a self-contained sub-project of ~5–10 lemmas.

63	## Steps that may proceed in parallel

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.LineBundle
7	
8	/-!
9	# The relative Picard functor
10	
11	Phase C step 2 (per `STRATEGY.md`): the relative Picard functor of a curve
12	`C` over `Spec k`, packaged as a contravariant functor on schemes-over-`k`,
13	plus the (deferred) representability theorem whose closure jointly unblocks
14	the four `Jacobian.lean` sorries.
15	
16	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_Functor.tex`.
17	
18	## Status (iteration 005 — first prover round)
19	
20	`PicardFunctor` is **filled**: for `C : Over (Spec k)` the functor sends each
21	`S : Over (Spec k)` to the cokernel-as-quotient
22	`Pic(C ×_k S) / p_S^* Pic(S)` of the pull-back homomorphism along the
23	projection `p_S : C ×_k S → S`. Functoriality in `S` is via the universal map
24	of fiber products and `Pic.pullback`.
25	
26	`PicardFunctor.representable` is intentionally left as `sorry` — see the
27	forward-compatibility note below.
28	
29	## Forward-compatibility note (`LineBundle` approximation)
30	
31	`LineBundle` (per `Picard/LineBundle.lean`) is currently realised as the
32	global-sections approximation `CommRing.Pic Γ(X, ⊤)`. The relative Picard
33	functor built on top of this approximation gives smaller subgroups than the
34	true relative Picard functor on non-affine `S`. Closing `representable` on
35	top of this approximation would silently assert representability of the wrong
36	functor and is therefore a forbidden shortcut: keep it as `sorry`.
37	
38	## Source category
39	
40	The directive's sketch had source `Schemeᵒᵖ`, but a generic `S : Scheme` does
41	not come equipped with a structure morphism to `Spec k` — without it the fiber
42	product `C ×_k S` is not canonically defined. Following the blueprint's
43	mathematical setup (`Sch_k = Sch / Spec k`) and the convention already used in
44	`Jacobian.lean`, we therefore take the source as `(Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of
45	k)))ᵒᵖ`, i.e.\ contravariant on `k`-schemes. This preserves the intended
46	functor while making the fiber product canonically defined; the
47	`IsRepresentable` predicate then asks for a representing object inside
48	`Over (Spec k)`, matching the geometric meaning of the Picard scheme as a
49	`k`-scheme. See `task_results/Picard_Functor.lean.md` for the rationale.
50	-/
51	
52	set_option autoImplicit false
53	
54	universe u
55	
56	open CategoryTheory Limits Opposite
57	
58	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
59	
60	variable {k : Type u} [Field k]
61	
62	namespace PicardFunctor
63	
64	/-- The map on fiber products `C ×_k S₁ → C ×_k S₂` induced by
65	`f : S₁ ⟶ S₂` in `Over (Spec k)`, given by the universal property of pullback
66	applied to `(𝟙 C, f.left)`. -/
67	noncomputable def fiberMap (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
68	    {S₁ S₂ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₁ ⟶ S₂) :
69	    Limits.pullback C.hom S₁.hom ⟶ Limits.pullback C.hom S₂.hom :=
70	  Limits.pullback.map _ _ _ _ (𝟙 _) f.left (𝟙 _) (by simp)
71	    (by rw [Category.comp_id]; exact (Over.w f).symm)
72	
73	@[simp, reassoc]
74	lemma fiberMap_comp_snd (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
75	    {S₁ S₂ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₁ ⟶ S₂) :
76	    fiberMap C f ≫ Limits.pullback.snd C.hom S₂.hom =
77	      Limits.pullback.snd C.hom S₁.hom ≫ f.left := by
78	  unfold fiberMap; exact Limits.pullback.lift_snd _ _ _
79	
80	@[simp, reassoc]

80	@[simp, reassoc]
81	lemma fiberMap_comp_fst (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
82	    {S₁ S₂ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₁ ⟶ S₂) :
83	    fiberMap C f ≫ Limits.pullback.fst C.hom S₂.hom =
84	      Limits.pullback.fst C.hom S₁.hom := by
85	  unfold fiberMap; rw [Limits.pullback.lift_fst]; simp
86	
87	@[simp]
88	lemma fiberMap_id (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
89	    (S : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) :
90	    fiberMap C (𝟙 S) = 𝟙 _ := by
91	  unfold fiberMap; exact Limits.pullback.map_id
92	
93	lemma fiberMap_comp (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
94	    {S₁ S₂ S₃ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₁ ⟶ S₂) (g : S₂ ⟶ S₃) :
95	    fiberMap C (f ≫ g) = fiberMap C f ≫ fiberMap C g := by
96	  refine Limits.pullback.hom_ext ?_ ?_
97	  · simp
98	  · simp only [fiberMap_comp_snd, Category.assoc, Over.comp_left, fiberMap_comp_snd_assoc]
99	
100	/-- The pull-back homomorphism on the relative Picard quotient
101	`Pic(C ×_k S₁) / p_{S₁}^* Pic(S₁) → Pic(C ×_k S₂) / p_{S₂}^* Pic(S₂)` induced
102	by `f : S₂ ⟶ S₁` in `Over (Spec k)`.
103	
104	The descent through the quotient uses the commutative square
105	`fiberMap C f ≫ pullback.snd = pullback.snd ≫ f.left`
106	(`fiberMap_comp_snd`), which sends `p_{S₁}^* Pic(S₁)` into `p_{S₂}^* Pic(S₂)`
107	via `Pic.pullback (fiberMap C f)`. -/
108	noncomputable def quotMap (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
109	    {S₁ S₂ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₂ ⟶ S₁) :
110	    (Pic (Limits.pullback C.hom S₁.hom) ⧸
111	        (Pic.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd C.hom S₁.hom)).range) →*
112	      (Pic (Limits.pullback C.hom S₂.hom) ⧸
113	        (Pic.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd C.hom S₂.hom)).range) :=
114	  QuotientGroup.lift _
115	    ((QuotientGroup.mk' _).comp (Pic.pullback (fiberMap C f)))
116	    (by
117	      rintro x ⟨m, rfl⟩
118	      simp only [MonoidHom.mem_ker, MonoidHom.coe_comp, Function.comp_apply,
119	        QuotientGroup.mk'_apply]
120	      rw [QuotientGroup.eq_one_iff]
121	      refine ⟨Pic.pullback f.left m, ?_⟩
122	      rw [← MonoidHom.comp_apply (Pic.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd C.hom S₂.hom)),
123	          ← Pic.pullback_comp, ← fiberMap_comp_snd, Pic.pullback_comp,
124	          MonoidHom.comp_apply])
125	
126	@[simp]
127	lemma quotMap_mk (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
128	    {S₁ S₂ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₂ ⟶ S₁)
129	    (x : Pic (Limits.pullback C.hom S₁.hom)) :
130	    quotMap C f (x : _ ⧸ _) = ((Pic.pullback (fiberMap C f) x) : _ ⧸ _) := rfl
131	
132	@[simp]
133	lemma quotMap_id (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
134	    (S : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) :
135	    quotMap C (𝟙 S) = MonoidHom.id _ := by
136	  ext x; simp
137	
138	lemma quotMap_comp (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
139	    {S₁ S₂ S₃ : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))} (f : S₂ ⟶ S₁) (g : S₃ ⟶ S₂) :
140	    quotMap C (g ≫ f) = (quotMap C g).comp (quotMap C f) := by
141	  ext x
142	  simp [fiberMap_comp, Pic.pullback_comp]
143	
144	end PicardFunctor
145	
146	/-- The relative Picard functor of `C` over `Spec k`:
147	    `S ↦ Pic(C ×_k S) / p_S^* Pic(S)`,
148	where `p_S : C ×_k S → S` is the projection. Functoriality in `S` is via base
149	change of the fiber product (`PicardFunctor.fiberMap`) and the pull-back
150	homomorphism on Picard groups (`Pic.pullback`).
151	
152	**Iteration 005 implementation.** The fiber product is taken in
153	`Limits.pullback C.hom S.unop.hom`, the projection `p_S = pullback.snd`, and
154	the quotient by the pull-back range uses `QuotientGroup.lift` /
155	`QuotientGroup.mk'`. See `PicardFunctor.fiberMap` and `PicardFunctor.quotMap`
156	for the underlying constructions and the source-category note in the file
157	docstring. -/
158	noncomputable def PicardFunctor
159	    (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))) :
160	    (Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u where
161	  obj S := Pic (Limits.pullback C.hom S.unop.hom) ⧸
162	    (Pic.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd C.hom S.unop.hom)).range
163	  map {_ _} f := TypeCat.ofHom fun x ↦ PicardFunctor.quotMap C f.unop x
164	  map_id _ := by
165	    apply TypeCat.Hom.ext; apply TypeCat.Fun.ext
166	    funext x
167	    change PicardFunctor.quotMap C (𝟙 _) x = x
168	    simp
169	  map_comp f g := by
170	    apply TypeCat.Hom.ext; apply TypeCat.Fun.ext
171	    funext x
172	    change PicardFunctor.quotMap C (f ≫ g).unop x =
173	      PicardFunctor.quotMap C g.unop (PicardFunctor.quotMap C f.unop x)
174	    rw [unop_comp, PicardFunctor.quotMap_comp]; rfl
175	
176	/-- Representability of the relative Picard functor for a smooth, proper,
177	geometrically irreducible curve `C` over a field `k` (Grothendieck FGA, Mumford
178	*Abelian Varieties* §III.13). The connected component of the identity of the
179	representing group scheme is the Jacobian `Jac(C)`.
180	
181	**Intentionally deferred.** This is FGA-level and not honestly closeable on
182	the global-sections-approximate `LineBundle`. The four `Jacobian.lean` sorries
183	all reduce to this theorem. Do not attempt to fill it in iter-005 — see the
184	file docstring and `Picard_Functor.tex` forward-compatibility note. -/
185	theorem PicardFunctor.representable
186	    (C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)))
187	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
188	    [IsProper C.hom]
189	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] :
190	    (PicardFunctor C).IsRepresentable := sorry
191	
192	end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
